boundary_records.boundary_statement: Acute, subacute, and chronic windows must not be merged unless a study directly tracks the same model across time.
Contusion, compression, transection, crush, hemisection, ischemia, and chronic human lesions produce different lesion trajectories.
Static chronic lesion anatomy belongs in Module 1 unless the row explains formation.
Inflammatory cells can mediate damage, debris clearance, corralling, repair signaling, or chronic pathology depending on timing and state.
Astrocytic and fibrotic scar processes can both protect tissue continuity and restrict regeneration.
Rodent lesion formation cannot be directly mapped to human chronic anatomy without scale, severity, and tissue-preservation boundaries.
Acute neuroprotection or lesion-size reduction is not equivalent to chronic reconstruction.
Module 13 consensus must remain a causal bridge and should not duplicate intervention modules.

consensus_drafts.draft_statement: Primary traumatic SCI sets an initial lesion geometry, but the chronic lesion is produced by a time-dependent cascade rather than by the mechanical insult alone; injury model, severity, spinal level, and early hemorrhage must be retained as causal boundaries.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Primary traumatic SCI sets an initial lesion geometry, but the chronic lesion is produced by a time-dependent cascade rather than by the mechanical insult alone; injury model, severity, spinal level, and early hemorrhage must be retained as causal boundaries.

consensus_drafts.draft_statement: Vascular failure, blood-spinal cord barrier disruption, edema, and progressive hemorrhagic necrosis are early drivers of lesion expansion and are experimentally modifiable, making vascular integrity a core injury-to-lesion mechanism.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Vascular failure, blood-spinal cord barrier disruption, edema, and progressive hemorrhagic necrosis are early drivers of lesion expansion and are experimentally modifiable, making vascular integrity a core injury-to-lesion mechanism.

consensus_drafts.draft_statement: Gray matter loss can occur rapidly while white matter loss, axon degeneration, demyelination, and Wallerian degeneration evolve over longer windows, so chronic spared-tissue anatomy must be interpreted through time-resolved tract and cell-population boundaries.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Gray matter loss can occur rapidly while white matter loss, axon degeneration, demyelination, and Wallerian degeneration evolve over longer windows, so chronic spared-tissue anatomy must be interpreted through time-resolved tract and cell-population boundaries.

consensus_drafts.draft_statement: Innate immune responses after traumatic SCI are not only damaging inflammation: microglia and macrophages can expand injury, clear debris, compact wounds, corral lesion cores, and alter chronic neurodegeneration depending on timing and state.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Innate immune responses after traumatic SCI are not only damaging inflammation: microglia and macrophages can expand injury, clear debris, compact wounds, corral lesion cores, and alter chronic neurodegeneration depending on timing and state.

consensus_drafts.draft_statement: Astrocytic border formation is an active wound-repair process that contains non-neural lesion cores and coordinates multicellular scar responses; it should not be summarized only as a regeneration-inhibitory barrier.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Astrocytic border formation is an active wound-repair process that contains non-neural lesion cores and coordinates multicellular scar responses; it should not be summarized only as a regeneration-inhibitory barrier.

consensus_drafts.draft_statement: Fibrotic scar formation is a spatially organized stromal response involving heterogeneous fibroblast/perivascular/meningeal compartments and extracellular matrix programs that help define the chronic lesion core and interface.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Fibrotic scar formation is a spatially organized stromal response involving heterogeneous fibroblast/perivascular/meningeal compartments and extracellular matrix programs that help define the chronic lesion core and interface.

consensus_drafts.draft_statement: Cavitation and chronic lesion stabilization emerge from interacting vascular, immune, glial, stromal, axon/myelin, and tissue-clearance processes; static chronic lesion anatomy should be linked back to the temporal mechanisms that formed it.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Cavitation and chronic lesion stabilization emerge from interacting vascular, immune, glial, stromal, axon/myelin, and tissue-clearance processes; static chronic lesion anatomy should be linked back to the temporal mechanisms that formed it.

consensus_drafts.draft_statement: Module 13 should serve as the causal bridge between model-system selection and chronic lesion architecture: every downstream conditioning, biomaterial, cell, or axon-repair claim should specify which injury-to-lesion process it is expected to modify.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Module 13 should serve as the causal bridge between model-system selection and chronic lesion architecture: every downstream conditioning, biomaterial, cell, or axon-repair claim should specify which injury-to-lesion process it is expected to modify.
